content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 11,
1994
Peter A.
Bagatelos
Bagatelos
& Fadem
601 California St.,
Ste.
1801
San Francisco,
CA
94108
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
1—93—450
Dear Mr.
Bagatelos:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act
(the “Act”) •1/
Because you have not identified the client on
whose behalf you have requested this advice,
we will treat your
letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)
2/
Your letter,
dated November 22,
1993, accurately summarizes
my advice that your corporate client will not receive a
contribution as defined in the Act by virtue of receiving
discounted legal services.
The discounted legal services were
offered by the law firm as an alternative to making a
contribution to the corporation’s sponsored ballot measure
committee and were not offered and will not be used by the
committee or the corporation in connection with the ballot measure
or for any other political purposes.
Under these circumstances,
the law firm’s services would not be deemed a contribution under
the Act.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Peter A.
Bagatelos
Our File No.
1—93-450
Page
2
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.conclusion: Your letter, dated November 22, 1993, accurately summarizes my advice that your corporate client will not receive a contribution as defined in the Act by virtue of receiving discounted legal services. The discounted legal services were offered by the law firm as an alternative to making a contribution to the corporation's sponsored ballot measure committee and were not offered and will not be used by the committee or the corporation in connection with the ballot measure or for any other political purposes. Under these circumstances, the law firm's services would not be deemed a contribution under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a corporate client receiving discounted legal services from a law firm (offered as an alternative to making a contribution to a ballot measure committee) constitutes a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that discounted legal services offered by a law firm as an alternative to a direct contribution to a ballot measure committee do not constitute a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act, provided the services are not used for political purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is confirmation of prior telephone advice treated as informal assistance request under Regulation 18329(c). No formal Q/C sections present, but advice is clearly stated. File number 1-93-450 from January 1994.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a corporate client receiving discounted legal services from a law firm (offered as an alternative to making a contribution to a ballot measure committee) constitutes a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Your letter, dated November 22, 1993, accurately summarizes my advice that your corporate client will not receive a contribution as defined in the Act by virtue of receiving discounted legal services. The discounted legal services were offered by the law firm as an alternative to making a contribution to the corporation's sponsored ballot measure committee and were not offered and will not be used by the committee or the corporation in connection with the ballot measure or for any other political purposes. Under these circumstances, the law firm's services would not be deemed a contribution under the Act.

The discounted legal services were offered by the law firm as an alternative to making a contribution to the corporation’s sponsored ballot measure committee and were not offered and will not be used by the committee or the corporation in connection with the ballot measure or for any other political purposes. Under these circumstances, the law firm’s services would not be deemed a contribution under the Act. 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Peter A. embedding.summary: This is an FPPC confirmation letter responding to a request for confirmation of telephone advice regarding whether discounted legal services constitute a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC confirms that such services do not constitute a contribution when offered as an alternative to a direct contribution and not used for political purposes.
